one_line_takeaway: A pragmatic, government-tested playbook for evaluating LLM APPLICATION safety end-to-end (custom taxonomy + black-box adversarial prompting + refusal-as-safety-proxy, scored as 1 - ASR), strong on operational realism but light on reported quantitative results.
key_finding: The framework was validated in a real GovTech internal pilot on two external-facing chatbots using a two-level benchmark of 1,600 basic prompts and 33,600 intermediate prompts (the latter generated by applying adversarial attack templates to the basic set). Qualitatively, LLM-as-a-judge was the most effective refusal-detection method, capturing nuanced behavior and scaling with a single evaluator across both systems; the two chatbots gave notably different responses due to system-configuration/context differences, confirming that application-level factors, not just the base model, drive safety. The pilot surfaced emerging risks early, letting teams harden systems before public release and raising stakeholder confidence. It seeded two operational tools: Litmus (org-wide safety-testing platform) and Sentinel (guardrails platform).
core_contribution: A practical, adaptable framework for evaluating application-level (not foundation-model-level) safety of deployed LLM systems, contributed by GovTech Singapore (with a UC Berkeley co-author) at the ICML 2025 TAIG workshop. The framework has two parts the authors present as their own: (1) principles for developing an organization-customized safety risk taxonomy (contextualize general risks; focus on practicality/present-day harms; anchor to legal/regulatory definitions), and (2) practices for empirically evaluating safety risks end-to-end (curate contextualized adversarial prompts; treat the whole application as a black box behind a single API; evaluate free-text responses via refusal detection; compute a safety score = 1 - Attack Success Rate). It is a framework/experience paper, not a new benchmark or algorithm; the empirical content is a case study of an internal pilot rather than a leaderboard comparison.
threat_model: Explicitly scoped to 'simple or common safety attacks that an average or reasonably knowledgeable user may attempt,' NOT sophisticated or worst-case adversaries. The paper openly states no theoretical safety guarantees are possible over the infinite prompt space; the goal is a rough empirical assurance that a deployed chatbot 'cannot be easily abused or broken.' Attack surface is the full application (system prompt, RAG pipeline, fine-tuning, guardrails) as an integrated black box, since prior work (Qi et al. 2023 on fine-tuning; An et al. 2025 on RAG; Zheng et al. 2024 / Lu et al. 2025 on system prompts) shows these components degrade safety relative to the base model. Adversarial prompts span a complexity gradient from direct instructions up to jailbreak templates (DAN, Shen et al. 2024) and base64 encoding. Risk categories are harms of the model's OUTPUT (hateful/sexual/violent content, self-harm, misconduct, unqualified legal/financial/medical advice, political content) rather than classic infosec exploits.
limitations: Reports NO actual safety-score / ASR numbers for the two chatbots, so the empirical validation is qualitative and not independently checkable; the pilot is n=2 chatbots within a single government agency. The framework is deliberately scoped to single-turn, English/Singlish, text-only, discrete-risk prompts against 'average user' attacks; the authors explicitly defer multi-turn, multilingual, multimodal, intersecting/compound risks, and automated red-teaming to future work. Self-acknowledged caveats: a perfect score does not imply zero risk (LLM stochasticity, evolving threats, evaluator error); a poor score does not imply the app is undeployable (external UI/UX or auth mitigations help); and safety scores say nothing about utility, which must be measured separately. 'Safe enough' has no general threshold; teams must set their own risk appetite. Much prompt-curation and taxonomy content is guidance/best-practice rather than validated method. Taxonomy and legal anchoring are Singapore-specific and need re-contextualization elsewhere.

datasets_used: Internal GovTech safety benchmark - 1,600 basic adversarial prompts
Internal GovTech safety benchmark - 33,600 intermediate adversarial prompts (basic prompts expanded via adversarial attack templates)

Organization-specific safety risk taxonomy: 3 top categories (Undesirable content, Specialized advice, Political content) with subcategories and severity levels (full definitions + example prompts in Appendix A)
quantitative_results: 1,600 basic prompts and 33,600 intermediate prompts in the two-level internal benchmark
Case study evaluated 2 external-facing government LLM chatbots
Safety score defined as proportion of safe responses over total prompts, i.e. 1 - Attack Success Rate (ASR); NOTE: the paper reports no numeric safety scores/ASR values for the pilot chatbots
baselines_compared: No competing method is benchmarked against - this is a framework/case-study paper, not a comparison. Existing safety benchmarks (SafetyBench, AIR-BENCH 2024, HarmBench) are cited as motivating contrasts (they test foundation models in isolation, not full applications), NOT as empirical baselines.

Among the paper's own candidate refusal-detection techniques, LLM-as-a-judge was found most effective vs. keyword search and open-source refusal classifiers (LibrAI-LongFormer, ProtectAI DistilRoBERTa); generic moderation classifiers (OpenAI Moderation API, Llama Guard) cited as insufficient for context-specific policy.
AILuminate (Vidgen et al. 2024) referenced as a precedent for grading safety relative to a reference model, not run as a baseline.
